Flood:
DISASTER TYPE 02
Flood
River overflow, flash floods, and coastal inundation — the world's most frequent disaster
Quick Facts & Classification
Onset
Slow-onset (river flood): hours to days; Flash flood: minutes to 1 hour
Duration
Hours to weeks depending on type and drainage capacity
Severity indicators
Flood depth, velocity, debris load, urban vs rural terrain
Primary hazards
Drowning, hypothermia, waterborne disease, structural collapse
Secondary hazards
Landslide, dam/levee failure, power outages, contaminated water supply
Most affected
Low-lying coastal and riverine communities; informal settlements without drainage
Global median deaths
16 per event (but mean is 1,735 due to extreme outliers)
Key terms
flood inundation flash flood river overflow waterlogging submerged drowning
Before: Preparedness Actions
These actions should be completed before a disaster event occurs. Preparedness is the most effective
way to reduce injury, death, and economic loss.
• Know your flood zone. Check official flood hazard maps for your address. Flood zones change —
re-check every 5 years or after major upstream development.
• Elevate electrical panels, HVAC systems, and appliances at least 30cm above the local base flood
elevation. This single measure dramatically reduces flood damage.
• Install check valves in plumbing to prevent sewage from backing up into your home during floods.
• Keep important documents in a waterproof container or upload scanned copies to cloud storage. Include
insurance policies, property deeds, passports, and medical records.
• Prepare a flood emergency kit: waterproof torch, life jackets for all household members, rope (15m
minimum), first aid kit, water purification tablets, 3-day food and water supply, portable phone charger.
• Know multiple evacuation routes — floodwaters can cut off roads rapidly. Identify routes to high ground
that do not cross low-lying bridges.
• Identify and pre-arrange an out-of-area contact person who can serve as the family communication hub.
• Keep sandbags available if you live in a frequently flooded area. Learn proper sandbagging technique —
incorrectly placed sandbags are ineffective.
• Obtain flood insurance well in advance — standard property insurance does not cover flood damage in
most countries.
• Trim trees and clear drainage ditches, gutters, and downspouts regularly to prevent blockage.

During: Immediate Response Actions
These are the actions to take while the disaster event is actively occurring. Prioritise personal safety above
all else.
• Move immediately to higher ground when a flash flood warning is issued. Do not wait to observe the
flood — by the time water is visible, escape routes may already be cut off.
• Never attempt to walk through moving water. Just 15cm of fast-moving water can knock an adult off their
feet. 30cm of water can sweep away a vehicle.
• Never drive through flooded roads. 'Turn around, don't drown' — most flood deaths occur in vehicles.
Water depth is impossible to judge on a road surface.
• If your vehicle is caught in rising water: exit immediately if water has not yet entered the cabin. If
trapped, open a window and exit as soon as possible before water pressure makes doors impossible to
open.
• Disconnect electrical appliances. Do not touch electrical equipment in wet conditions or if standing in
water.
• If trapped on an upper floor or roof: signal rescuers with bright cloth, torch, or whistle. Do not attempt to
swim unless in immediate danger from rising water.
• Avoid contact with floodwater — it may contain sewage, chemicals, and sharp debris.
• If outdoors and caught in a flash flood, climb to higher ground — seek a tree, hill, or elevated structure.
Do not enter culverts, drainage ditches, or storm channels.
After: Recovery Actions
These actions apply in the hours, days, and weeks following the disaster event. Recovery involves
physical safety, health, and psychological wellbeing.
• Do not return home until authorities declare it safe. Water may still be rising in some areas, and
structural damage may not be visible.
• Assume all tap water is contaminated until officially cleared. Drink only bottled or boiled water. Use water
purification tablets if available.
• Wear rubber boots and waterproof gloves when entering flooded buildings. Floodwater carries
pathogens, chemicals, and sharp objects.
• Document all damage with photographs and video before cleaning. Notify your insurance company
immediately.
• Discard all food that has come into contact with floodwater, including canned goods with dented or
bulging lids.
• Dry out your home within 24-48 hours to prevent mould growth. Use fans and dehumidifiers. Mould can
cause severe respiratory illness.
• Check for structural damage: cracks in foundations, warped floors, and shifted walls indicate unsafe
structures.
• Watch for signs of waterborne disease in the following days: diarrhoea, vomiting, fever, skin rashes.
Seek medical care promptly.
• Report downed power lines, broken gas mains, and road damage to authorities immediately.
• Complete an emotional recovery check with all family members — flooding is traumatic, especially for
children.

Medical Guidance
• Drowning and near-drowning: all near-drowning victims require medical evaluation even if they appear
recovered — delayed pulmonary oedema can be fatal.
• Wound infection: floodwater lacerations have a very high infection rate including tetanus. All wounds
must be thoroughly cleaned and assessed by a medical professional.
• Waterborne diseases: leptospirosis, typhoid, hepatitis A, and cholera are common post-flood diseases.
Symptoms appear 2-30 days after exposure. Seek medical care for any fever or gastrointestinal
symptoms.
• Hypothermia: immersion in cold water reduces body core temperature rapidly. Remove wet clothing,
wrap in dry blankets, and seek medical care.
• Carbon monoxide poisoning: portable generators used indoors or in garages after floods cause many
post-flood deaths. Always run generators outdoors, at least 6m from any opening.
Evacuation Procedures
• Leave as soon as a flood WATCH is issued — do not wait for a WARNING. Early departure avoids traffic
gridlock and road inundation.
• Take your emergency kit, medications, and important documents.
• Disconnect utilities at the main switches before leaving if time permits.
• Lock your home and follow the official evacuation route. Inform your out-of-area contact of your
destination.
• If evacuating with pets, identify pet-friendly shelters or arrangements in advance — most emergency
shelters do not accept animals.
Emergency Contacts & Reporting
• National Meteorological Service — official flood warnings and river level monitoring
• Local civil defence authority — evacuation orders, shelter locations
• Red Cross / Red Crescent — emergency shelter, food, and family tracing
• Local water authority — water safety announcements post-flood
CRITICAL WARNING: Flash floods kill in minutes
Flash floods are the deadliest form of flooding. The time from first warning to life-threatening flood depth
can be under 10 minutes. Treat every flash flood warning as immediate. Never seek shelter in
ground-floor rooms, underground car parks, or basements during a flash flood warning.
SURVIVAL TIP: The 30-cm rule for vehicles
30cm of moving floodwater is enough to float and sweep away a mid-size car. If the road ahead shows
any standing water of uncertain depth, reverse and find an alternate route. No journey is worth a
vehicle-related flood death.

Statistical Profile — Flood
Floods are the most frequent natural disaster globally: 5,551 events (1900–2021). Median deaths: 16 per
event. Median affected: 11,000 — the highest of all disaster types. Median damage: $49.5M USD. Flood
events increased from 524 (1980s) to 1,725 (2000s) — a 3.3x increase over 20 years, driven by
urbanisation and climate change.
Storm:
DISASTER TYPE 13
Storm
Tropical cyclones, hurricanes, typhoons, severe thunderstorms, and tornadoes
Quick Facts & Classification
Types
Tropical cyclone/hurricane/typhoon, extratropical cyclone, severe thunderstorm, tornado,
hailstorm, blizzard, sandstorm
Onset
Tropical cyclones: days of warning available; Tornadoes: minutes of warning;
Thunderstorms: hours
Duration
Tornadoes: minutes; Thunderstorms: hours; Hurricanes: days; Blizzards: days
Wind speed
thresholds
Tropical Storm: 63-118 km/h; Category 1 Hurricane: 119-153 km/h; Category 5: 252+
km/h
Primary hazards
Storm surge, high winds, flooding, tornadoes, lightning, hail
Secondary hazards
Power outages, coastal erosion, infrastructure destruction, freshwater contamination
Global median deaths
14 per event; major events kill thousands
Key terms
hurricane cyclone typhoon storm tornado thunderstorm wind storm surge blizzard hail
lightning
Before: Preparedness Actions
These actions should be completed before a disaster event occurs. Preparedness is the most effective
way to reduce injury, death, and economic loss.
• Know your location's storm risk: coastal zone (storm surge), tornado alley (tornadoes), or inland
(wind/flood). Different risks require different preparations.
• Reinforce your home for high winds: inspect and upgrade roof anchoring, install storm shutters or board
windows, and brace garage doors.
• TROPICAL CYCLONE: prepare a 72-hour emergency kit. Know your evacuation zone (A-F or 1-5) and
your route to an inland shelter.
• TORNADO: identify the safest location in your home — lowest floor, interior room, away from windows.
A basement is ideal. Pre-designate this location with all household members.
• LIGHTNING: install surge protectors for all electronics. Know the '30-30 rule': if the time between
lightning flash and thunder is less than 30 seconds, seek shelter; wait 30 minutes after the last thunder
before going outside.
• Clear your yard of loose items that can become projectiles in high winds: garden furniture, tools, pot
plants, and decorative items.
• Charge all devices and portable battery banks before a storm.
• Know how to shut off utilities at the mains. Have manual tools available for after a storm when power
tools may not be usable.

• Obtain storm insurance and document all property for claims.
• For coastal communities: know the storm surge risk. Storm surge, not wind, kills the majority of
hurricane victims.
During: Immediate Response Actions
These are the actions to take while the disaster event is actively occurring. Prioritise personal safety above
all else.
• TROPICAL CYCLONE: shelter in a pre-identified interior room on the lowest floor if not evacuating. Stay
away from windows. Do not go outside during the eye of the storm — conditions will deteriorate again
rapidly.
• TORNADO WARNING: seek shelter immediately in the lowest interior room or basement. Protect head
and neck with arms. Do not seek shelter under an overpass — wind speeds are higher there.
• TORNADO — if in a vehicle: abandon the vehicle and seek shelter in a solid building. If no building is
available: lie flat in a ditch or low-lying area away from trees and vehicles, covering head and neck.
• LIGHTNING: indoors is safest. Stay away from tall isolated trees, metal fences, open water, and hilltops.
If caught outdoors: crouch low, stay away from tall objects, spread out (5m between group members),
and remove metal backpacks.
• STORM SURGE: evacuate all surge zones as directed. Storm surge of 3-6 metres can overtop sea
walls and travel kilometres inland. It is the deadliest component of a hurricane.
• Stay informed through official emergency broadcast radio. Do not rely solely on phone networks, which
may be overloaded or fail.
• Do not attempt to drive through flooded or debris-covered roads.
After: Recovery Actions
These actions apply in the hours, days, and weeks following the disaster event. Recovery involves
physical safety, health, and psychological wellbeing.
• Do not go outside until officially declared safe. Post-storm debris, downed power lines, and structural
hazards are immediate threats.
• Treat all downed power lines as live. Keep a 10-metre exclusion zone from all downed wires and report
immediately to the power utility.
• Document all damage with photographs before cleanup, for insurance and government assistance
claims.
• Do not use portable generators, charcoal grills, or camp stoves indoors — carbon monoxide poisoning
kills many people in the days after major storms.
• Be aware of mould growth in flood-affected structures — remove wet materials within 24-48 hours.
• Conduct a welfare check on all vulnerable neighbours immediately after the storm.
• Use text messages for communication — voice networks are typically congested after major storms.
• Do not consume tap water until the supply is officially declared safe.
Medical Guidance

• Carbon monoxide poisoning from indoor generator use is the most preventable post-storm cause of
death. Symptoms: headache, dizziness, confusion, nausea. Evacuate immediately and call emergency
services if suspected.
• Chainsaw and debris-clearing injuries: post-storm cleanup is one of the most dangerous periods. Use
proper PPE (gloves, eye protection, steel-capped boots), never operate chainsaws alone, and rest
regularly.
• Storm surge drowning: the primary cause of hurricane mortality. Treat all water immersion casualties as
potential near-drowning and hypothermia cases requiring medical evaluation.
• Lightning strike: all lightning strike victims require urgent medical evaluation even if they appear
uninjured. Secondary cardiac effects can develop hours after the strike.
Evacuation Procedures
• Tropical cyclone evacuation orders are mandatory in many jurisdictions. Comply immediately and early
— late evacuation can be impossible once conditions deteriorate.
• Know your evacuation zone designation and the shelter locations for your zone before storm season.
• If you choose to shelter in place (for Category 1-2 hurricanes), ensure your structure is rated for the
expected wind speed and you are not in a storm surge zone.
• Evacuate before storm conditions arrive. Evacuating through tropical cyclone conditions is extremely
dangerous.
Emergency Contacts & Reporting
• National Meteorological Service — official storm warnings and tracks
• National Emergency Management Agency — evacuation orders and shelter locations
• Red Cross — storm shelter, food, and family reunification services
• Power utility emergency line — downed power lines and outage reporting
CRITICAL WARNING: Storm surge is the deadliest hurricane hazard — not the wind
In major hurricanes, storm surge — ocean water pushed inland by the storm's rotation — can reach 6
metres or more and penetrate many kilometres inland. Even a Category 1 hurricane can produce a
deadly storm surge. Wind speed ratings do not reflect storm surge risk. Evacuate all coastal storm surge
zones as directed, regardless of wind category.
SURVIVAL TIP: Your safest place in a tornado is underground
A basement or below-grade safe room provides the best tornado protection. Interior rooms on the lowest
floor are the next best option. The absolute worst locations are mobile homes (always evacuate before a
tornado threat), areas under bridges and overpasses (creates a wind tunnel effect), and vehicles. Drill
your plan before storm season.
Statistical Profile — Storm

Storms are the second most frequent natural disaster : 4,496 events (1900–2021). Median deaths: 14
per event. Median damage: $76M USD — the second highest after extreme temperature. Storm activity
and intensity is increasing with ocean warming. The 1970 Bhola Cyclone (Bangladesh, 500,000 deaths)
remains the deadliest storm on record.
Earthquake:
DISASTER TYPE 01
Earthquake
Tectonic ground shaking — sudden, violent, no advance warning
Quick Facts & Classification
Onset
Sudden — seconds to minutes
Duration
Seconds to 2 minutes (main shock); hours to months (aftershocks)
Severity scale
Richter / Moment Magnitude (Mw); MMI for shaking intensity
Primary hazards
Ground shaking, surface rupture, liquefaction, landslides, tsunamis
Secondary hazards
Fire, dam failure, building collapse, infrastructure failure
Most affected
Urban areas on soft sediment; regions near tectonic plate boundaries
Global median deaths
20 per event (global); up to 87,000 in major events
Key terms
earthquake seismic tremor aftershock magnitude shaking structural collapse
Before: Preparedness Actions
These actions should be completed before a disaster event occurs. Preparedness is the most effective
way to reduce injury, death, and economic loss.
• Conduct a structural assessment of your building for seismic vulnerability. Hire a licensed engineer to
retrofit unreinforced masonry or weak soft-story structures.
• Secure heavy furniture (bookshelves, water heaters, refrigerators) to walls using anti-tip straps and
L-brackets. Place heavy objects on lower shelves.
• Identify safe spots in every room: under sturdy tables, against interior walls away from windows. Practice
Drop-Cover-Hold drills with all household members every 6 months.
• Prepare a Go-Bag containing: 72-hour water supply (4 litres per person per day), non-perishable food,
first aid kit, copies of ID documents, cash, medications, torch, whistle, dust masks (N95), and a
portable radio.
• Know how to shut off gas, water, and electricity at the main valves. Keep a wrench near the gas meter.
Label all shut-off points clearly.
• Store emergency supplies in multiple locations (home, car, workplace). Do not rely on a single cache.
• Identify your local emergency assembly point and evacuation routes. Share the plan with all family
members including children and elderly.
• Elevate water heaters and large appliances off the floor. Use flexible gas connectors approved for
seismic zones.
• Keep shoes and a torch under your bed. Broken glass is the most common cause of foot injuries after an
earthquake.

• Obtain earthquake insurance where available. Photograph and document all property for insurance
claims.
During: Immediate Response Actions
These are the actions to take while the disaster event is actively occurring. Prioritise personal safety above
all else.
• DROP immediately to hands and knees. This protects you from being knocked down and allows you to
move if needed.
• Take COVER under a sturdy desk or table, or against an interior wall. Cover your head and neck with
your arms.
• HOLD ON until the shaking stops. Move with the table if it shifts.
• If no table is available, crouch against an interior wall, covering your head and neck with both arms. Stay
away from exterior walls, windows, and anything that could fall.
• If outdoors: move away from buildings, streetlights, and utility wires. Once in the open, drop to the
ground and stay there.
• If in a vehicle: pull over away from buildings, bridges, overpasses, and power lines. Stay inside with
seatbelt fastened until shaking stops.
• If in a coastal area: immediately after shaking, move inland and to higher ground — a tsunami may
follow within minutes.
• Do NOT use elevators during or immediately after shaking.
• Do NOT run outside during shaking — most injuries occur when people try to move or run.
• Do NOT stand in doorways — this is an outdated myth. A doorframe offers no more protection than the
rest of the structure.
After: Recovery Actions
These actions apply in the hours, days, and weeks following the disaster event. Recovery involves
physical safety, health, and psychological wellbeing.
• Expect and prepare for aftershocks. They can occur minutes, days, or months after the main event and
can be strong enough to collapse weakened structures.
• Check yourself and others for injuries. Provide first aid. Do not move seriously injured people unless
they are in immediate danger.
• Check for gas leaks: smell for gas, listen for hissing. If suspected, open windows, leave immediately,
and call the gas company from outside.
• Check for structural damage before re-entering your building. Look for cracks in walls, shifted
foundations, and warped doors.
• Turn off electricity at the main breaker if you see sparks or damaged wiring. Do not use candles or open
flames if gas leaks are possible.
• Do not use tap water until authorities confirm it is safe — water mains may be cracked.
• Tune in to official emergency broadcasts for damage reports, road closures, and shelter locations.
• Document all damage with photographs before cleaning up, for insurance and government assistance
claims.

• Use text messages rather than voice calls to reduce network congestion. Social media check-ins can
help families confirm safety.
• If displaced, go to official shelters. Do not enter red-tagged (unsafe) buildings under any circumstances.
Medical Guidance
• Crush syndrome: people trapped under rubble may develop life-threatening kidney failure when
suddenly freed. Alert rescue teams — do not rapidly pull someone free without medical standby.
• Wound infection: debris-related wounds carry high infection risk. Clean all wounds thoroughly with clean
water and apply antiseptic. Seek medical care for deep lacerations.
• Hypothermia: if trapped overnight, conserve body heat using any available materials — clothing,
cardboard, insulation.
• Psychological trauma: acute stress reactions are normal. Provide calm reassurance. Children and
elderly are especially vulnerable. Seek mental health support in the days and weeks following the
event.
• Do not give food or water to an unconscious person. Place them in the recovery position if breathing.
Evacuation Procedures
• Evacuate only when authorities issue an official order or when your structure is clearly unsafe.
• Follow designated evacuation routes — do not use bridges or overpasses that may be structurally
compromised.
• Drive slowly and watch for road damage, downed power lines, and sinkholes.
• If you smell gas anywhere along your route, do not use lighters or matches. Avoid the area and report it.
• Do not return home until authorities declare the area safe.
Emergency Contacts & Reporting
• National Emergency Management Agency (NEMA) — your country's primary earthquake emergency
authority
• Local fire department — gas leaks, structural fires, trapped persons
• Red Cross / Red Crescent — shelter, family reunification, emergency relief
• Seismological monitoring centre — aftershock updates and magnitude reports
CRITICAL WARNING: NEVER re-enter a red-tagged building
A red tag means professional engineers have assessed the structure as unsafe. Re-entry risks fatal
collapse during aftershocks. Respect all official tagging regardless of the perceived urgency of retrieving
belongings.
SURVIVAL TIP: Whistle — your best survival tool

A loud whistle requires far less energy than shouting and can be heard by rescuers through rubble. Keep
one in your Go-Bag, on your keyring, and under your bed. Three blasts is the universal distress signal.
Statistical Profile — Earthquake
Earthquakes account for 1,544 recorded events (1900–2021). Median deaths: 20 per event globally.
Median affected: 4,050. Median damage: $38.8M USD. Insurance coverage is only 17% — the lowest of
all disaster types. Richter magnitude is the key impact predictor: Mw 7.0+ events have a 60x higher
median death toll than Mw 5.0-6.0 events.
Wildfire:
DISASTER TYPE 15
Wildfire
Uncontrolled vegetation fires — increasingly frequent and severe due to climate change
Quick Facts & Classification
Onset
Rapid — a wildfire can travel faster than a person can run (6+ km/h in light fuel; up to 23
km/h with wind)
Primary drivers
Drought, high temperatures, low humidity, wind, accumulated dry fuel (vegetation),
ignition source
Types
Crown fire (treetop), surface fire, ground fire (peat), interface fire (wildland-urban)
Primary hazards
Direct flame, radiant heat, smoke inhalation, ember spotting (fires ignited by airborne
embers ahead of the fire front)
Secondary hazards
Post-fire landslide (destabilised slopes), flooding (destroyed vegetation), air quality crisis
Global median
damage
$102M USD — third highest of all disaster types
Global median deaths
6 per event (but major events kill hundreds)
Key terms
wildfire bushfire forest fire vegetation fire smoke ember spot fire evacuation
Before: Preparedness Actions
These actions should be completed before a disaster event occurs. Preparedness is the most effective
way to reduce injury, death, and economic loss.
• Create a DEFENSIBLE SPACE around your home: remove dry vegetation within 10m of your structure;
thin and trim vegetation between 10-30m; clear roofs, gutters, and decks of leaves and debris.
• Use fire-resistant building materials on roofs, walls, and decks where possible. Block all ember entry
points: gaps in eaves, roof vents, and under doors.
• Store firewood, propane tanks, and flammable materials away from your home structure (minimum
10m).
• Keep your gutters clean of dry leaves — burning embers landing in gutters filled with dry material ignite
homes before the fire front arrives.
• Prepare your home for rapid departure: store your Go-Bag in your vehicle, park facing the exit direction,
and keep your fuel tank at least half full during fire season.
• Know your community's fire emergency plan: evacuation routes (multiple, in case one is blocked by fire),
designated assembly points, and shelter locations.
• Identify your local warning system: emergency alerts, sirens, and how local authorities issue evacuation
orders.
• Monitor fire danger ratings daily during fire season. Know what a 'Catastrophic' or 'Code Red' rating
means for your region.

• Ensure all household members know the plan and can execute it independently, including children.
• Obtain home and contents insurance that includes wildfire coverage. Photograph all contents.
During: Immediate Response Actions
These are the actions to take while the disaster event is actively occurring. Prioritise personal safety above
all else.
• Evacuate as early as possible when a warning is issued. Late evacuation in the path of a rapidly
advancing fire is fatal.
• If ordered to evacuate: go immediately. Do not wait to collect additional belongings. Your life is not worth
any object.
• If you cannot evacuate (trapped): shelter in the most fire-resistant building available. Close all doors and
windows. Fill sinks and baths with water. Remove all curtains and move furniture away from windows.
• Smoke is the primary immediate danger: close all windows and doors to reduce smoke infiltration. Seal
gaps with wet towels.
• Avoid driving through smoke — smoke causes rapid and complete loss of visibility. If caught in dense
smoke while driving, pull off, turn off the engine, close all vents, and stay in the vehicle.
• Do not shelter in your vehicle as a last resort in the path of a fire front — a vehicle's fuel tank, tyres, and
plastic components can ignite. Seek a structure or cleared area.
• If all other options are exhausted and fire overruns your location: lie face-down in a depression or ditch
on cleared ground, covering your body with non-synthetic clothing or soil.
• Monitor your official emergency radio or app continuously — fire conditions change rapidly and
evacuation orders may be issued with very short notice.
After: Recovery Actions
These actions apply in the hours, days, and weeks following the disaster event. Recovery involves
physical safety, health, and psychological wellbeing.
• Do not return home until officially declared safe — fire can reignite in smouldering ground or structures
for days after the main front passes.
• Assume all tap water is unsafe until your water authority confirms otherwise — wildfires melt and
contaminate plastic water pipes.
• Wear a P2/N95 mask when re-entering fire-affected areas — ash and soil from burned areas contain
toxic compounds including heavy metals.
• Wear protective gloves, long sleeves, and closed-toe shoes during post-fire cleanup.
• Burned slopes are highly susceptible to landslide and flooding for years after a fire — the protective root
structure of vegetation has been destroyed. Follow rainfall warnings diligently.
• Document all damage with photographs and video before any cleanup, for insurance claims and
government assistance.
• Check in with your mental health: wildfire survivors experience high rates of PTSD, anxiety, and grief.
Seek support.
• Check air quality index (AQI) before spending time outdoors in fire-affected regions — smoke can cause
serious respiratory harm at levels well below visible haze.

Medical Guidance
• Smoke inhalation: carbon monoxide and hydrogen cyanide in wildfire smoke are rapidly lethal at high
concentrations. Move casualties to fresh air immediately. Seek emergency medical care for all
significant smoke exposure — delayed pulmonary oedema can develop hours later.
• Burns: do not apply ice, butter, or toothpaste to burns. Cool with clean cool (not cold) running water for
20 minutes. Cover with a clean non-stick dressing. Seek immediate medical care for all but the most
minor burns.
• Eye irritation from ash and smoke: flush eyes with clean water. Avoid rubbing. Wear goggles when in
smoke or ash conditions.
• Post-fire air quality illness: fine particulate matter (PM2.5) from wildfire smoke causes and exacerbates
asthma, COPD, and cardiovascular disease. Those with pre-existing conditions should monitor AQI
and shelter indoors during high-PM periods.
Evacuation Procedures
• Evacuate on WATCH level — do not wait for an ORDER. Roads in fire zones can be cut off by fire,
accidents (due to mass evacuation traffic), or downed power lines.
• Know multiple exit routes from your community. Practice your primary and alternate routes before fire
season.
• If your route is blocked by fire and you cannot turn back: do not drive through the fire. Seek the nearest
structure or open cleared area.
• Pet and livestock evacuation requires advance planning — arrange transport and destination in
advance. Do not delay your own evacuation for animals if you have no plan.
Emergency Contacts & Reporting
• National fire authority — fire danger ratings, incident updates, and evacuation orders
• Local emergency management — official evacuation orders and shelter locations
• Emergency broadcast radio — the most reliable source of information during power outages
• Red Cross — emergency shelter and family reunification
CRITICAL WARNING: Ember attack ignites homes — not the fire front
In most wildland-urban interface fires, homes are not ignited by the main fire front directly. They are
ignited by burning embers (firebrands) that travel ahead of the fire on the wind, landing on roofs, in
gutters, and in gaps in eaves. This is why defensible space and ember-proofing your home matters far
more than the fire's distance from your property. A home 2km from a fire front can be ignited by embers
in minutes.
SURVIVAL TIP: Leave early — never late

Every wildfire fatality analysis reaches the same conclusion: people who died trying to evacuate waited
too long. A fire that is 20km away under dry, hot, windy conditions can reach your home in under an
hour. When a watch is issued, load your car and be ready. When an order is issued, leave immediately
without debate. The cost of an unnecessary evacuation is one night in a shelter. The cost of a late
evacuation can be your life.
Statistical Profile — Wildfire
Global disaster records show 471 wildfire events (1900–2021). Median damage: $102M USD — third
highest of all disaster types. The 2019-2020 Australian Black Summer fires burned 18.6 million hectares,
killed 34 people directly, and an estimated 417 people from smoke-related illness. California's 2018
Camp Fire (85 deaths, $16.5B damage) illustrates the catastrophic potential of wildland-urban interface
fires.
Volcanic activity:
DISASTER TYPE 14
Volcanic Activity
Eruptions, pyroclastic flows, ash fall, and lahars — geological hazards near active
volcanoes
Quick Facts & Classification
Onset
Variable — from sudden phreatic explosions (no warning) to months of precursor activity
Hazard types
Lava flow, pyroclastic flow/surge, ash fall, lahar (volcanic mudflow), volcanic gas, ballistic
projectiles, tsunami (flank collapse)
Zones
Exclusion zone (life-threatening), restricted zone (high risk), caution zone (ash and gas
hazard)
Key regions
Pacific Ring of Fire, East African Rift, Iceland, Caribbean arc
Global median deaths
40 per event — second highest after drought
Most dangerous
hazard
Pyroclastic flow — superheated gas and debris at 700°C travelling at 700+ km/h
Key terms
volcano eruption lava pyroclastic ash fall lahar volcanic gas sulfur dioxide volcanic alert
Before: Preparedness Actions
These actions should be completed before a disaster event occurs. Preparedness is the most effective
way to reduce injury, death, and economic loss.
• Know the volcanic alert level system used by your national volcanological authority (typically:
Normal/Green, Advisory/Yellow, Watch/Orange, Warning/Red).
• Know the hazard zones for all volcanoes near your community — maps are available from national
geological surveys.
• Prepare a Go-Bag, including N95 masks (for ash fall) and goggles.
• Keep vehicles fuelled during periods of elevated volcanic alert — ash fall can make roads impassable
within hours.
• Secure your water supply: volcanic ash contaminates open water sources rapidly. Store water in sealed
containers.
• Know the lahar risk for your area: lahars (volcanic mudflows) travel along river valleys and can occur
during or long after an eruption when rain mobilises volcanic deposits. Do not assume a lahar risk has
passed because the eruption has ended.
• Understand eruption alert level thresholds and what actions are required at each level. Know your
community's designated evacuation assembly points.
During: Immediate Response Actions

These are the actions to take while the disaster event is actively occurring. Prioritise personal safety above
all else.
• Follow all official evacuation orders immediately. Volcanic hazard zones are determined by expert
volcanological analysis — do not assume the exclusion zone is excessive.
• ASH FALL: remain indoors with windows and doors closed. Wear an N95 mask if you must go outside.
Cover nose and mouth with a damp cloth if a mask is unavailable. Protect eyes with goggles.
• ASH FALL: clear ash from rooftops regularly — wet ash is extremely heavy (up to 1,000 kg/m2) and can
cause structural roof collapse. Do not allow ash to accumulate.
• LAHAR WARNING: move immediately to high ground — lahars travel at 20-40 km/h along valley floors
and are virtually impossible to outrun if caught on the valley floor.
• LAVA FLOW: evacuate as directed. Lava moves slowly enough to evacuate from, but it destroys
everything in its path absolutely and irreversibly.
• PYROCLASTIC FLOW: if in the path of a pyroclastic flow, there is no survivable shelter in its direct path.
The only survival strategy is to not be in the exclusion zone when an eruption occurs.
• VOLCANIC GAS (sulfur dioxide, hydrogen sulfide): seek higher ground — volcanic gases are denser
than air and accumulate in low-lying areas, particularly valleys and caves.
• Monitor your official alert radio continuously. Alert levels can change rapidly.
After: Recovery Actions
These actions apply in the hours, days, and weeks following the disaster event. Recovery involves
physical safety, health, and psychological wellbeing.
• Ash fall cleanup: use N95 masks and goggles. Do not use water to clean ash from rooftops (it becomes
concrete-like when wet). Use dry sweeping.
• Do not consume food or water that has been exposed to volcanic ash until tested or treated.
• Follow official guidance on water safety — volcanic ash contaminates surface water supplies and can
block municipal water infrastructure.
• Lahar risk persists for years after an eruption. Heavy rainfall can mobilise volcanic deposits long after
the eruption has ceased. Maintain ongoing awareness of lahar warning systems.
• Structures in ash-fall areas must be assessed for structural integrity before re-entry — ash loading can
cause roof collapse.
• Monitor respiratory health in the weeks following ash fall — volcanic ash causes serious respiratory
conditions including silicosis with repeated exposure.
Medical Guidance
• Ash inhalation: volcanic ash particles are sharp, chemically reactive, and can penetrate deep into the
lungs. Prolonged exposure without respiratory protection causes silicosis. Anyone with significant ash
inhalation should receive medical evaluation.
• Eye injury: volcanic ash particles cause corneal abrasion. Flush eyes immediately with clean water. Use
goggles in ash-fall conditions.
• Burns: lava and pyroclastic deposits remain thermally hazardous for extended periods. Do not approach
recently deposited volcanic material without specialist equipment.

• Volcanic gas poisoning (H2S): hydrogen sulfide smells of rotten eggs at low concentrations but causes
olfactory fatigue at higher concentrations — you stop smelling it before it kills you. Evacuate any area
where H2S is suspected without relying on your sense of smell.
Evacuation Procedures
• Evacuate when ordered — do not wait to observe the eruption. Many volcanic eruption fatalities occur
among those who delayed evacuation.
• Follow designated routes. Avoid river valleys in lahar risk areas.
• Do not attempt to observe the eruption from 'safe' vantage points near the exclusion zone — eruption
intensification can be sudden.
• Carry your N95 masks, goggles, and Go-Bag. Evacuation may be prolonged — months to years in
severe cases.
Emergency Contacts & Reporting
• National volcanological observatory — official eruption alerts and exclusion zone status
• Civil defence and emergency management — evacuation coordination
• National health authority — ash health guidance and medical support
CRITICAL WARNING: There is no shelter from a direct pyroclastic flow
A pyroclastic flow is a superheated mixture of gas, ash, and volcanic rock at up to 700 degrees Celsius,
travelling at up to 700 km/h. No building, vehicle, or structure can withstand a direct pyroclastic flow. The
1902 eruption of Mount Pelee killed 30,000 people in minutes. The only survival strategy is to be outside
the exclusion zone before the eruption.
SURVIVAL TIP: Clear ash from your roof — wet ash weighs as much as concrete
Volcanic ash accumulating on a roof to a depth of just 10-15 cm can weigh as much as 100-150 kg per
square metre. After heavy ash fall, many building roofs collapse under this load, particularly flat or
shallow-pitched roofs. Clear ash regularly during an ash fall event using a broom. Do not add water —
wet ash is far heavier.
Statistical Profile — Volcanic Activity
Global disaster records show 265 volcanic events (1900–2021). Median deaths: 40 per event — the
second highest of all disaster types. The 1815 Tambora eruption (Indonesia, 71,000+ deaths and
subsequent global climate impact) remains the most impactful volcanic event in recorded history.
Modern volcano monitoring has dramatically improved evacuation lead times.
Landslide:
DISASTER TYPE 11
Landslide
Mass movement of earth, rock, and debris — triggered by rainfall, earthquakes, and
deforestation
Quick Facts & Classification
Onset
Rapid to sudden — debris flows can travel at 20-30 m/s; slow creep landslides move
centimetres per year
Trigger factors
Heavy or prolonged rainfall, seismic activity, volcanic activity, deforestation, slope
cutting, saturation
Types
Rockfall, debris flow, mudslide, slump, translational slide, rotational slide
Primary hazards
Burial, trauma, asphyxiation, river damming and subsequent flood
Secondary hazards
Landslide dam outburst floods, road and infrastructure destruction, gas and power line
rupture
Global median deaths
27 per event; individual events can kill hundreds
Key terms
landslide mudslide debris flow rockfall slope failure mass movement slope collapse
Before: Preparedness Actions
These actions should be completed before a disaster event occurs. Preparedness is the most effective
way to reduce injury, death, and economic loss.
• Know whether your home is on or downslope of landslide-susceptible terrain. Look for warning signs:
cracked walls, tilting trees and poles, unusual sounds, and water seeping from slopes.
• Contact your local geology or civil engineering authority for slope stability assessments in high-risk
areas.
• Maintain or replant vegetation on slopes above your home — root systems dramatically improve slope
stability.
• Avoid building on, near, or at the base of steep slopes, near drainage channels, or on natural landslide
deposits.
• Install French drains or surface drainage to divert water away from steep slopes adjacent to your
property.
• Know your evacuation route to safe ground. Ensure it does not cross drainages or the base of
susceptible slopes.
• Keep a Go-Bag with essential items, medications, and documents.
During: Immediate Response Actions

These are the actions to take while the disaster event is actively occurring. Prioritise personal safety above
all else.
• If you receive a landslide warning or observe warning signs (rumbling sounds, cracking of ground,
flowing water carrying increasing sediment): evacuate to higher ground immediately.
• If landslide is imminent and escape is impossible: curl into a tight ball, protect your head with your arms,
and move to the strongest part of the building (typically an interior corner).
• If caught in a debris flow: try to escape perpendicular to the flow direction — run to the side, not downhill.
• Stay out of valleys, drainage channels, and low-lying areas during and immediately after intense rainfall,
especially in landslide-prone areas.
• Do not attempt to cross a debris-covered road or bridge until it has been inspected by engineers.
• Listen for changes in sounds: a roaring or rumbling that increases in volume typically indicates an
approaching debris flow.
After: Recovery Actions
These actions apply in the hours, days, and weeks following the disaster event. Recovery involves
physical safety, health, and psychological wellbeing.
• Stay away from the landslide area — additional slides are likely, particularly in the 24-72 hours following
the initial event.
• Check for injured and trapped persons. Do not enter unstable areas to rescue without proper equipment
and training.
• Report the landslide to local emergency management. Provide location, size, and whether persons are
trapped.
• Check for and report broken gas, electrical, and water lines.
• Do not attempt to re-enter buildings that were in the path of or adjacent to the landslide until structurally
assessed.
• Landslide dams can form rapidly in river channels — if a valley river suddenly stops flowing, a landslide
dam may have formed upstream. Evacuate immediately — outburst flooding can follow with little
warning.
Medical Guidance
• Crush syndrome: persons buried under debris may develop life-threatening kidney failure when
released. Alert medical teams — do not rapidly extract buried persons without medical standby.
• Asphyxiation: debris can rapidly compress the chest. Even persons with their head above debris may be
unable to breathe. Rapid, careful excavation of the chest is the priority.
• Hypothermia: wet, buried conditions rapidly reduce core body temperature. Insulate and warm the victim
once excavated.
• Traumatic injuries from debris impact are common and may be masked by shock. All landslide survivors
should receive medical evaluation.
Evacuation Procedures
• Evacuate immediately upon landslide warning — do not attempt to collect belongings.

• Follow routes that go away from slopes, not along valley floors.
• Establish a family meeting point on high, stable ground away from the affected slope system.
Emergency Contacts & Reporting
• National geological survey — landslide hazard maps and warnings
• Local civil defence — evacuation orders and rescue coordination
• Emergency services — rescue of trapped persons
CRITICAL WARNING: A landslide dam can burst without warning
When a landslide blocks a river, a dam forms. This dam can hold back weeks of water — and fail
suddenly, releasing a catastrophic flood wave. If a river in a landslide-affected area stops flowing or
reverses, immediately evacuate all areas downstream of the blockage. Do not wait for official
confirmation.
SURVIVAL TIP: Cracks in your walls or ground may be your earliest warning
Slow-moving landslides begin long before the catastrophic phase. New cracks in walls, tilting of trees
and poles, doors and windows that no longer close properly, and ground that appears to have shifted are
early warning signs of slope instability. Report these observations to your local civil engineering authority
immediately.
Statistical Profile — Landslide
Global disaster records show 776 landslide events (1900–2021). Median deaths: 27 — among the
highest. Landslides are triggered by many other disaster types (earthquake, volcanic, excessive rainfall)
and are classified as secondary hazards in many events. Deforestation dramatically increases landslide
risk — studies show up to 20x higher slide frequency in deforested vs forested slopes.
Drought:
DISASTER TYPE 04
Drought
Prolonged water deficit — the silent, slow-onset disaster with the largest affected
populations
Quick Facts & Classification
Onset
Slow — weeks to months to years
Duration
Months to years; some droughts persist for decades
Types
Meteorological (rainfall deficit), agricultural (soil moisture), hydrological
(groundwater/river), socioeconomic
Primary hazards
Food insecurity, malnutrition, water scarcity, livestock loss, wildfire risk
Secondary hazards
Mass displacement, civil conflict, epidemic disease, economic collapse
Global median
affected
758,000 per event — the highest of any disaster type
Global median deaths
143 per event (highest median of all disaster types)
Key terms
drought water scarcity food insecurity dry spell arid desertification famine malnutrition
Before: Preparedness Actions
These actions should be completed before a disaster event occurs. Preparedness is the most effective
way to reduce injury, death, and economic loss.
• Monitor official drought indicators: Standardised Precipitation Index (SPI), Palmer Drought Severity
Index, and seasonal rainfall forecasts from national meteorological services.
• Implement water conservation immediately when drought watches are issued: fix all leaks, reduce
landscape irrigation, install water-efficient fixtures, and collect rainwater where legally permitted.
• Develop a household water budget. Know your daily per-person minimum requirement: 15 litres for
basic survival, 50+ litres for health and dignity.
• Diversify food sources and maintain emergency food stocks covering at least 3 months. Prioritise
calorie-dense, non-perishable items.
• For agricultural communities: shift to drought-resistant crop varieties, implement drip irrigation, and
mulch soil to retain moisture.
• Know the location of alternative water sources: standpipes, water distribution points, NGO distribution
schedules.
• Register vulnerable household members (elderly, infants, chronically ill) with local health authorities for
priority access to drought relief programmes.
• Create or join a community water committee to coordinate equitable distribution of scarce water
resources.

During: Immediate Response Actions
These are the actions to take while the disaster event is actively occurring. Prioritise personal safety above
all else.
• Strictly ration all water use. The WHO minimum for drinking and sanitation is 15 litres per person per
day. Prioritise drinking water, then hygiene, then food preparation.
• Use every drop: collect cooking water for cleaning, use grey water from washing for gardens (non-food
plants).
• Prioritise vulnerable persons: infants, pregnant and breastfeeding women, the elderly, and those with
chronic illness have the highest water and nutrition needs.
• Monitor children for signs of dehydration and malnutrition: sunken eyes, poor skin turgor, irritability,
reduced urine output, weight loss. Seek medical care immediately.
• Protect remaining water sources from contamination. Cover wells, label all storage containers, and
prevent animals from accessing water storage points.
• Avoid strenuous outdoor activity during peak heat hours (typically 10:00-16:00). Stay in shade or cool
areas.
• For livestock keepers: destocking early is more economically advantageous than waiting for animals to
die. Coordinate with agricultural extension officers on appropriate destocking strategies.
• Be alert to signs of social tension around water access. Conflict over scarce water is a documented
drought-related risk.
After: Recovery Actions
These actions apply in the hours, days, and weeks following the disaster event. Recovery involves
physical safety, health, and psychological wellbeing.
• When rainfall returns, do not immediately consume rainwater without testing or treating it — first rains
after a prolonged drought can mobilise accumulated pollutants and pathogens from dry soil.
• Rehydration after severe dehydration should be gradual — oral rehydration solution (ORS) is safer than
plain water for severely dehydrated individuals, especially children.
• Re-establish food gardens and agricultural production as soon as soil moisture permits, but prioritise
fast-growing food crops over cash crops in the initial recovery period.
• Monitor for post-drought epidemic diseases: cholera, typhoid, and diarrhoeal diseases surge after
droughts when compromised hygiene meets recovering water supply systems.
• Assess psychological impacts on all household members, especially children and those who lost
livestock or crops. Drought-related economic loss is a major driver of depression and family
breakdown.
• Apply for government and NGO drought relief and agricultural recovery assistance. Document all losses
(livestock, crops, property) for claims.
• Invest recovery resources in long-term drought resilience: water harvesting systems, drought-resistant
crops, and local water storage infrastructure.
Medical Guidance
• Severe dehydration in children: give Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) — not plain water alone — in
small, frequent sips. Seek medical care if the child cannot drink, has bloody diarrhoea, or deteriorates.

• Heat stroke: a core body temperature above 40°C with altered consciousness is a medical emergency.
Cool the person immediately with any available method (water, shade, fanning) and seek emergency
medical care.
• Malnutrition: mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) is the key screening tool. A MUAC below 11.5cm in
children 6-59 months indicates severe acute malnutrition requiring therapeutic feeding.
• Mental health: drought-related livelihoods loss is a leading driver of rural suicide in affected populations.
Take all expressions of hopelessness seriously and connect people to mental health support.
Evacuation Procedures
• Evacuation during drought is primarily driven by food and water insecurity reaching critical levels.
• If water supply is completely exhausted, move to the nearest water distribution point or relief camp
without delay.
• Organise community transport for those who cannot self-evacuate, particularly the elderly and disabled.
• Register at official displacement camps — this ensures access to food and water distribution, health
services, and family tracing.
Emergency Contacts & Reporting
• National Meteorological Service — drought watch and rainfall forecast updates
• Ministry of Agriculture — destocking support, seed distribution, crop insurance
• UN World Food Programme (WFP) — food assistance in major drought events
• UNICEF — nutrition support for children and mothers
CRITICAL WARNING: Do not ignore early drought warning signals
Drought mortality is highest when communities and governments fail to act on early warning indicators. A
3-month delay in emergency response typically doubles the affected population requiring therapeutic
feeding. Act on the first official drought watch — do not wait for declaration of emergency.
SURVIVAL TIP: ORS saves lives — prepare it correctly
Oral Rehydration Solution: dissolve 6 level teaspoons of sugar and 0.5 teaspoon of salt in 1 litre of clean
water. This simple solution prevents death from dehydration. Prepare it fresh daily and give to those
showing dehydration symptoms before reaching medical care.
Statistical Profile — Drought
Droughts affect more people than any other disaster type: median 758,000 per event. Global disaster
records show 770 drought events (1900–2021). Deaths data is sparse (9% coverage) but median deaths
of 143 reflects famine-related mortality. The 1983 Ethiopia drought caused ~3 million deaths, the largest
single-event death toll in the dataset.
Extreme temperature:
DISASTER TYPE 06
Extreme Temperature
Heatwaves and severe cold events — silent killers that disproportionately affect the elderly
Quick Facts & Classification
Onset
Gradual over hours to days (but effects can become critical rapidly)
Types
Heatwave / heat dome; Cold wave / freeze; Wind chill
Duration
Days to weeks
Primary hazards
Heat stroke, heat exhaustion, hypothermia, frostbite
Most vulnerable
Elderly, infants, outdoor workers, athletes, chronically ill, those without air conditioning or
heating
Global median deaths
38 per event; 2003 European heatwave killed ~70,000
Global median
damage
$200M USD — highest of all disaster types
Key terms
heatwave extreme heat cold wave hypothermia frostbite heat stroke heat exhaustion
temperature
Before: Preparedness Actions
These actions should be completed before a disaster event occurs. Preparedness is the most effective
way to reduce injury, death, and economic loss.
• HEAT: Identify the coolest locations in your community: air-conditioned public buildings, cooling centres,
and shaded parks. Know their addresses and operating hours.
• HEAT: Install reflective window coverings or shade structures. Dark curtains absorb heat — use
light-coloured, reflective materials.
• HEAT: Check on elderly neighbours and relatives daily during heatwave conditions. Isolation combined
with extreme heat is fatal.
• COLD: Insulate your home before winter: seal draughts around doors and windows, insulate pipes in
unheated spaces, and maintain your heating system annually.
• COLD: Prepare a cold emergency kit: blankets, warm clothing, hand warmers, non-perishable food,
candles, matches, and a battery-powered radio.
• ALL: Know your local meteorological service's alert thresholds for extreme temperature warnings. Sign
up for automated alerts.
• ALL: Review all medications — many drugs affect heat and cold tolerance: diuretics, beta-blockers,
antipsychotics, antihistamines, and alcohol.
• ALL: Maintain adequate hydration throughout extreme temperature events. Thirst is a late indicator of
dehydration.

During: Immediate Response Actions
These are the actions to take while the disaster event is actively occurring. Prioritise personal safety above
all else.
• HEAT: Stay indoors in the coolest available space during the hottest hours (10:00-16:00). Avoid direct
sun and physical exertion.
• HEAT: Drink water regularly — 2-3 litres per day minimum, more if active. Avoid alcohol, caffeine, and
sugary drinks, which accelerate dehydration.
• HEAT: Wear loose, light-coloured, breathable clothing. Cover exposed skin and wear a wide-brimmed
hat outdoors.
• HEAT: Use wet cloths, cool showers, and fans to lower body temperature. Mist spray bottles are highly
effective.
• HEAT: Never leave children, elderly, or pets in parked vehicles — interior temperatures can reach 70°C
within minutes even on mild days.
• COLD: Stay indoors. If you must go out, dress in dry, insulating layers (avoid cotton next to skin), cover
all extremities, and limit exposure time.
• COLD: Eat calorie-rich food — your body burns more energy maintaining core temperature in cold. Stay
hydrated — cold suppresses thirst sensation.
• COLD: If you detect shivering, confusion, slurred speech, or extreme fatigue in yourself or others —
these are early hypothermia signs. Seek warmth immediately.
After: Recovery Actions
These actions apply in the hours, days, and weeks following the disaster event. Recovery involves
physical safety, health, and psychological wellbeing.
• Check on vulnerable neighbours and community members as temperatures normalise.
• Seek medical evaluation for anyone who experienced severe heat or cold symptoms, even if they
appear to have recovered.
• Replenish water and food supplies consumed during the event.
• Report deaths and severe illness associated with extreme temperatures to public health authorities —
these events are frequently under-recorded.
• Assess your home for damage: frozen and burst pipes are a major post-cold-wave cause of property
damage. Check your insurance coverage.
Medical Guidance
• HEAT STROKE (core temp above 40°C + altered consciousness): medical emergency. Cool
immediately by any available method — immerse in cool water, wet clothing plus fanning. Call
emergency services. This is life-threatening.
• HEAT EXHAUSTION (heavy sweating, weakness, cold and pale skin, weak pulse, nausea): move to
cool environment, loosen clothing, apply cool wet cloths, give water. If symptoms progress to confusion
or loss of consciousness, treat as heat stroke.
• HYPOTHERMIA (core temp below 35°C): move the person to a warm environment gently. Remove wet
clothing. Cover with blankets. Give warm (not hot) liquids if conscious. Do not rub limbs vigorously. Call
emergency services for severe hypothermia.

• FROSTBITE: do not rub frozen tissue. Rewarm only if there is no risk of refreezing. Rewarm in warm
(37-40°C) water. Do not apply direct heat. Seek medical care — frostbite can result in tissue loss and
amputation.
Evacuation Procedures
• If home heating fails during a severe cold event, evacuate to a warming centre, community centre, or
hotel.
• If home cooling is inadequate during a severe heatwave, evacuate to an air-conditioned public building
or cooling centre.
• Assist neighbours without transportation. Community members dying alone in their homes is the primary
cause of extreme temperature mortality.
Emergency Contacts & Reporting
• National Meteorological Service — extreme temperature warnings
• Local emergency management — cooling and warming centre locations
• Emergency medical services — heat stroke and severe hypothermia are medical emergencies
CRITICAL WARNING: Elderly people may not feel extreme heat or cold accurately
Ageing impairs thermoregulation and thirst sensation. Elderly persons frequently do not report feeling too
hot or too cold even when their core temperature is at dangerous levels. Check on elderly relatives and
neighbours proactively — do not rely on them to self-report distress. Touch their skin and look for signs
of confusion or extreme lethargy.
SURVIVAL TIP: The fan-plus-wet-skin technique is more effective than fan alone
A fan alone in extreme heat can accelerate fluid loss without significantly cooling. Mist exposed skin with
water and then use a fan — evaporative cooling reduces skin temperature far more effectively than dry
air movement. In extreme heat above 35°C, a fan alone may increase heat stress.
Statistical Profile — Extreme Temperature
Global disaster records show 603 extreme temperature events (1900–2021). Median deaths: 38 per
event — one of the highest. Median damage: $200,000 USD per event — the highest of all disaster
types. The 2003 European heatwave (70,000 deaths) and 2010 Russian heatwave (56,000 deaths)
represent the deadliest modern events. Deaths are massively underreported.
